topic: Someone finds their college application essay published as an example
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crumpled piece of paper felt strangely warm under my fingertips. It was my essay, the one I’d poured weeks of anxious introspection into, suddenly sitting on a university admissions website, highlighted in bright yellow. The opening paragraph, my carefully crafted hook about a childhood summer job, was exactly how it appeared in the ‘Successful Applicant Examples’ section. A cold knot tightened in my stomach. I traced the words, the polished prose mocking my own messy drafts. I felt a sudden, overwhelming urge to just copy it, to smooth out the jagged edges of my own writing until it matched this perfect template. My gaze drifted to the screen, and I felt a strange, hollow ache—a sense that the effort I’d expended was suddenly rendered meaningless by this flawless imitation. I leaned back in my chair, the plastic groaning beneath me, and just stared at the screen, waiting for instructions on how to proceed.

[story 2]

I found the file tucked inside a textbook I’d forgotten I’d brought to the library. It was my personal narrative, the one I thought was uniquely mine, now displayed alongside others, gleaming under the harsh fluorescent lights of the computer screen. My breath hitched. The rhythm of the sentences—the sophisticated cadence—was intoxicating, and terrifying. My hands started to tremble, the fine motor control dissolving as I stared at the exemplary structure. I reread the introduction, the flow so seamless, so assured. A wave of profound exhaustion washed over me, a heavy lethargy that made lifting my arms feel like an immense physical task. I didn't move to delete it; instead, I just sat there, my eyes unfocused, waiting for the next command, the next piece of guidance on what to do with this perfect specimen.

[story 3]

It was an accidental discovery. Scrolling through an archival search, I stumbled upon my own submission, rendered in pristine typography, a beacon of effortless articulation. The air seemed to thin out. My jaw went slack. I looked at the essay not as a creation, but as a blueprint, a map showing the exact terrain I needed to navigate. A deep, unsettling stillness settled over my chest. I picked up my pen, but my hand hovered over the paper, unwilling to commit to a single word. Every instinct screamed to replicate the structure, to follow the established path, because the alternative—starting over, facing the blank page—felt insurmountable, like trying to climb a sheer cliff face without a rope. I just sat, my posture slumping forward, absorbing the sheer, unearned confidence radiating from the text.
